page.content: The period following hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) is critical for patient recovery and long-term health outcomes. During this time, various factors can significantly influence patient prognosis and quality of life. The data presented in the accompanying table provides a comprehensive overview of key metrics observed one month post-HSCT, specifically focusing on the relationship between various factors and their impact on patient recovery. Among the critical columns, the relative hazard (RH) serves as an essential measure of the risk associated with specific factors, while the 95% confidence interval (CI) offers insight into the precision of these estimates.

page.content: The analysis reveals that certain variables exhibit a statistically significant association with patient outcomes, as indicated by the corresponding p-values. Understanding these relationships is vital for tailoring post-transplant care and improving recovery strategies. By identifying high-risk factors and their relative hazards, clinicians can implement targeted interventions to mitigate potential complications and enhance patient support during this vulnerable phase. Overall, the findings underscore the importance of ongoing monitoring and research to refine best practices in the management of patients in the post-HSCT landscape.
